title: Speech Facing Quarter-End Pressures: Understanding the Repo Market and Federal Reserve Tools

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: Roberto Perli, Manager of the System Open Market Account (SOMA), delivered a speech explaining the Fed's monetary policy implementation framework and repo market dynamics. He emphasized that the framework of ample reserves with administered rates (IORB and ON RRP) has worked effectively since January 2019, keeping the EFFR within target range. Quarter-end repo rate pressures have increased since balance sheet runoff began in June 2022, but Perli stressed these pressures are not caused by reserve scarcity and remain within historical ranges observed in 2015-2017.
key_points: The Fed implements policy in an ample reserves regime where control over short-term rates is exercised through administered rates (IORB and ON RRP) rather than active reserve supply management
Since formal framework adoption in January 2019, the EFFR has only spent one day outside the target range

Repo rates have moved higher relative to administered rates over the past two years, driven by growing Treasury securities financing demand, reduced Fed liquidity from balance sheet runoff, and intermediation frictions
Quarter-end pressures since June 2022 are NOT induced by reserve scarcity—reserves remain abundant based on fed funds market pricing elasticity data
Most recent quarter-end (end of September 2024) saw SOFR peak roughly 21 basis points above its prior-week average
Historical comparison: temporary repo pressures of 10-25 bps were common in 2015-17 during a period of clearly abundant reserves, placing current pressures in normal historical context
The FHLBs' activity creates a channel for repo dynamics to influence fed funds rates and volumes
Standing Repo Facility (SRF) acts as a backstop to contain upward rate pressure and support smooth market functioning
why_it_matters: This speech provides authoritative Fed perspective on market plumbing that affects repo market participants, money market funds, and anyone reliant on short-term funding markets. Perli explicitly validates that quarter-end repo volatility is a structural feature, not a sign of systemic stress, and that the Fed's implementation framework remains robust. This is relevant for understanding QT endgame discussions and potential SRF modifications.
market_structure_context: The speech directly addresses market structure: Fed policy implementation framework (ample reserves regime), the role of IORB as a floor, ON RRP as a reinforcing floor, and SRF as a backstop. Perli explains how FHLB activity creates linkages between repo and fed funds markets. The discussion of quarter-end pressures highlights structural features of repo market functioning around financial reporting dates.
important_numbers: 21 bps - SOFR peak above prior-week average at most recent quarter-end (September 2024)
10-25 bps - historical range of temporary repo market pressures in 2015-17
June 2022 - balance sheet runoff start date cited for increased quarter-end pressures
January 2019 - formal adoption of ample reserves framework

issues_found: Source URL is empty in the record, though domain is correctly identified as newyorkfed.org
Source body is truncated mid-word at 'includi', cutting off discussion of intermediation frictions factors

notes: Source is a speech by Federal Reserve Bank of New York SOMA Manager Roberto Perli. Speech is primarily educational/explanatory about Fed monetary policy implementation tools and repo market dynamics rather than signaling new policy. The title in source metadata appears truncated. Speech content is directly relevant to market structure topics. Confirmed author is speaking in personal capacity ('views are my own and not necessarily those of either the Federal Reserve Bank of New York or the Federal Reserve System').
